1: rîbâ YHWH ʾet-yĕrîbay lĕḥam ʾet-lōḥāmāy (rîḇâ YHWH ʾeṯ-yĕrîḇay lĕḥam ʾeṯ-lōḥāmāy): 'Contend, O LORD, with those who contend with me; fight against those who fight against me.' Psalm 35 is an imprecatory lament: the psalmist asks YHWH to act as his legal advocate (rîb — 'to contend at law, plead a case') and military champion (lāḥam — 'to fight'). The verb rîb belongs to the legal sphere: a rîb is a formal dispute or lawsuit. The psalmist frames his enemies as parties in a lawsuit against him and asks YHWH to take up his side of the case. The parallel lōḥāmāy ('those who fight against me') adds the military dimension: the legal and military metaphors interweave throughout the psalm, as they do in divine warrior texts (Exod 15; Isa 49:25-26).
2: haḥăzēq māgēn wĕṣinnâ wĕqûmâ bĕʿezrātî (haḥăzēq māgēn wĕṣinnâ wĕqûmâ bĕʿezrātî): 'Take hold of shield and buckler and rise for my help!' The imperatives haḥăzēq ('take firm hold!') and qûmâ ('arise!') summon YHWH as divine warrior. The two shields — māgēn (small round shield) and ṣinnâ (large body-covering shield) — represent comprehensive military protection. The phrase qûmâ bĕʿezrātî, 'arise for my help,' uses qûmâ as the liturgical summons to divine action (cf. Pss 7:6; 9:19; 10:12; 27:3). The request is concrete: the warrior-God is to arm himself and stand between the psalmist and his attackers.
3: wĕhāreq ḥănît ûsgōr liqrāʾt rōdĕpāy (wĕhāreq ḥănîṯ ûs̱gōr liqrāʾṯ rōḏĕp̄āy): 'Draw out the spear and javelin against my pursuers.' The hiphil hāreq ('draw out, unsheath') applied to the ḥănît ('spear') continues the military arming of YHWH. The phrase ʾĕmōr lĕnapšî yĕšûʿātek ʾānî, 'say to my soul: I am your salvation' — the divine speech-act that transforms the inner person. The requested word is yĕšûʿātek ʾānî ('I am your salvation') — not merely 'I will save you' but an identity declaration: YHWH as the essence of salvation. The psalmist needs not just help but the assurance that his helper is salvation itself.
4: yēbōšû wĕyikklĕmû mĕbaqqĕšê napšî (yēḇōšû wĕyikkālĕmû mĕḇaqqĕšê napšî): 'Let them be put to shame and dishonored who seek my life!' The paired jussives — yēbōšû ('let them be ashamed') and yikklĕmû ('let them be dishonored/humiliated') — are imprecatory prayers for the reversal of the enemies' plot. The phrase mĕbaqqĕšê napšî ('those who seek my life') describes those seeking the napšî — the whole person, not merely physical life. The additional pair yissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyēḥpĕrû ('let them be turned back and confounded') amplifies the desired reversal: the attackers become the routed.
5: yihyû kĕmōṣ lipnê-rûaḥ ûmalʾak YHWH dôḥeh (yihyû kĕmōṣ lip̄nê-rûaḥ ûmalʾak YHWH dôḥeh): 'Let them be like chaff before the wind, with the angel of the LORD driving them away.' The chaff simile (kĕmōṣ lipnê-rûaḥ) pictures weight-lessness before power: the enemy who seemed substantial is revealed as insubstantial before the divine wind. The agent of their dispersal is malʾak YHWH ('the angel/messenger of the LORD') — the divine warrior's emissary who acts as enforcer of YHWH's judgments. The verb dôḥeh (hiphil participle from dāḥâ, 'to push, drive away') describes relentless pressure.
6: yĕhî darkām ḥōšek waḥălaqlaqqôt ûmalʾak YHWH rōdĕpām (yĕhî ḏarḵām ḥōšeḵ waḥălaqlaqqôṯ ûmalʾak YHWH rōḏĕp̄ām): 'Let their way be dark and slippery, with the angel of the LORD pursuing them.' The pairing of ḥōšek ('darkness') and ḥălaqlaqqôt ('slippery places') creates hazardous terrain without visible footing: the pursued cannot see where they step and the ground cannot be gripped. The angel of the LORD who was 'driving' (v.5) is now rōdĕpām ('pursuing') — the same verb used of the enemies' pursuit of the psalmist. The hunter becomes the hunted; divine agency reverses the predator-prey relationship.
7: kî ḥinnām ṭāmĕnû-lî šaḥat rišetām ḥinnām ḥāpĕrû lĕnapšî (kî ḥinnām ṭāmĕnû-lî šaḥaṯ rišetām ḥinnām ḥāp̄ĕrû lĕnapšî): 'For without cause they hid their net for me; without cause they dug a pit for my life.' The word ḥinnām ('without cause, for nothing, gratuitously') appears twice — a double emphasis on the unprovoked nature of the persecution. The two traps — rešet ('net') and šaḥat ('pit') — represent different modes of ambush. John 15:25 cites the word ḥinnām from this psalm: 'they hated me without cause' (combining Ps 35:19 and 69:4), applying the unprovoked hatred of the psalmist's enemies to the world's hatred of Jesus — the paradigmatic innocent sufferer.
8: tĕbôʾêhû šôʾâ lōʾ-yēdaʿ wĕrišetô ʾăšer ṭāman tilkĕdô (ṯĕḇôʾêhû šôʾâ lōʾ-yēdaʿ wĕrišetô ʾăšer ṭāman tilkĕḏô): 'Let destruction come upon him when he does not know it; let the net that he hid ensnare him.' The noun šôʾâ ('sudden ruin, catastrophe') — used of the day of devastation (cf. Isa 10:3; Zeph 1:15) — describes the unexpectedness of the wicked's doom: tĕbôʾêhû ... lōʾ-yēdaʿ ('let it come upon him without his knowing'). The lex talionis principle activates: wĕrišetô ʾăšer ṭāman tilkĕdô, 'let the net he hid catch him' — his own trap becomes his own capture. The planned ambush of v.7 returns on the planner (cf. Pss 7:15; 9:15-16).
9: wĕnapšî tāgîl baYHWH tāśîś bîšûʿātô (wĕnapšî ṯāḡîl ḇaYHWH ṯāśîś bîšûʿāṯô): 'Then my soul will rejoice in the LORD, exulting in his salvation.' The waw-consecutive wĕnapšî ('and my soul') signals the consequence of answered prayer: utter joy. The verb gîl ('rejoice, be glad') describes the bodily expression of spiritual elation; śîś ('exult, delight') adds intensity. Both are baYHWH and bîšûʿātô — in YHWH himself and in his yešûʿâ ('salvation'). The joy is not in the rescue alone but in the Rescuer: the act reveals the character of the one who acts, and the soul rejoices in the person discovered.
10: kol-ʿaṣmōtay tōʾmarnâ YHWH mî kāmôkā (ḵol-ʿaṣmōṯay ṯōʾmarnâ YHWH mî ḵāmôḵā): 'All my bones shall say, 'O LORD, who is like you?'' The phrase kol-ʿaṣmōtay ('all my bones') makes the entire skeleton — the structural self — a speaking chorus. The doxological question mî kāmôkā ('who is like you?') is the incomparability formula used throughout the OT (Exod 15:11; Pss 71:19; 89:8; Mic 7:18). The following words specify YHWH's incomparability: maṣṣîl ʿānî mēḥāzāq mimmennû wĕʿānî wĕʾebyôn mĕgōzlô, 'delivering the poor from one too strong for him, the poor and needy from one who robs him.' YHWH is uniquely the advocate of the structurally weak against the structurally powerful.
11: yĕqûmûn ʿēdê ḥāmās ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿtî yišʾālûnî (yĕqûmûn ʿēḏê ḥāmās ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḏaʿtî yišʾālûnî): 'Malicious witnesses rise up; they ask me of things I do not know.' The noun ʿēdê ḥāmās ('witnesses of violence') are perjurers — their testimony is not merely false but violent, designed to destroy. The phrase ʾăšer lōʾ-yādaʿtî yišʾālûnî, 'they ask me about things I do not know,' describes entrapment: questions about deeds never done or knowledge never possessed. The situation connects to the false witnesses against Jesus (Matt 26:60-61) and to the broader persecution motif that runs from the individual psalmist through to the Servant of Isaiah 53.
12: yĕšallĕmûnî rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ šĕkôl lĕnapšî (yĕšallĕmûnî rāʿâ taḥaṯ ṭôḇâ šĕḵôl lĕnapšî): 'They repay me evil for good, bereaving my soul.' The inversion rāʿâ taḥat ṭôbâ ('evil in place of good') is a recognized form of injustice throughout the OT: the ultimate perversion of reciprocity (cf. Prov 17:13; Ps 109:5; Rom 12:17). The noun šĕkôl ('bereavement, loss of children') describes the bereft state of a parent who has lost children — the most profound grief — applied to the napšî ('soul'): the psalmist's inner self is bereaved by the ingratitude of those he helped.
13: waʾănî bĕḥolotām lĕbûšî śāq ʿinnêtî batsôm napšî (waʾănî bĕḥolotām lĕḇûšî śāq ʿinnêṯî ḇaṣṣôm napšî): 'But I, when they were sick — I wore sackcloth; I afflicted myself with fasting.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but I') contrasts the enemies' ingratitude (v.12) with the psalmist's prior loyalty. The verbs — lābōš śāq ('wore sackcloth') and ʿinnâ bĕṣôm ('afflicted with fasting') — are mourning rites performed not for himself but for them: he fasted and wept over their illness. The phrase ûtĕpillātî ʿal-ḥêqî tāšûb, 'and my prayer returned to my bosom,' is difficult: either the prayer returned unanswered (possibly), or the psalmist's compassion turned back on himself (i.e., even his intercession for them produced personal grief).
14: kĕrēaʿ kĕʾāḥ-lî hiṯhallāktî kĕʾabel-ʾēm qōdēr šāḥôtî (kĕrēaʿ kĕʾāḥ-lî hiṯhallāḵtî kĕʾāḇel-ʾēm qōḏēr šāḥôtî): 'I went about as though I grieved for my friend or my brother; I bowed down in mourning as one who laments his mother.' The similes escalate: kĕrēaʿ ('like a friend') → kĕʾāḥ-lî ('like my own brother') → kĕʾabel-ʾēm ('like one mourning a mother'). The mourning gestures — qōdēr ('darkened, clad in dark garments') and šāḥôtî ('I bowed down') — enact grief publicly. The intensity of the mourning for the enemies' illness makes the enemies' ingratitude (v.12) all the more offensive: David mourned them like family, and they repaid him with violence.
15: ûbĕṣalʿî śāmĕḥû wĕneʾespû (ûḇĕṣalʿî śāmĕḥû wĕneʾespû): 'But at my stumbling they rejoiced and gathered.' The noun ṣālaʿ ('limping, stumbling') describes a momentary weakness or defeat — the psalmist's vulnerability. The response: śāmĕḥû ('they rejoiced') and neʾespû ('they gathered') — the gathering of mockers at the scene of another's pain. The phrase nēkîm śĕmûqîm ... lōʾ yādaʿtî, 'smiters whom I did not know gathered against me,' introduces anonymous attackers: not just known enemies but strangers who join in the assault. The social dimension is communal shame: public mockery from an assembled crowd.
16: bĕḥanpê lāʿăgê māʿôg ḥāraq ʿālay šinnêhem (bĕḥanp̄ê lāʿăḡê māʿôḡ ḥāraqqû ʿālay šinnêhem): 'Like profane mockers at a feast, they gnash their teeth at me.' The phrase ḥanpê lāʿăgê māʿôg ('godless jesters/mockers at a cake-feast') pictures those who mock at banquets — the context of contemptuous entertainment. The verb ḥāraš šinnayim ('gnash teeth') is the physical expression of rage and contempt (cf. Pss 37:12; 112:10; Lam 2:16). The enemies' enjoyment of the psalmist's suffering is a feast: his pain is their entertainment. The verb māʿôg ('cake, flat cake') may refer specifically to revelers who mock over food — those whose full stomachs enable their cruelty.
17: ʾădōnāy kammâ tirʾeh hāšîbâ napšî miššōʾêhem (ʾăḏōnāy kammâ tirʾeh hāšîḇâ napšî miššōʾêhem): 'O Lord, how long will you look on? Rescue my soul from their destruction.' The address ʾădōnāy ('my Lord') combined with kammâ tirʾeh ('how long will you see/watch?') is the lament of divine inaction: YHWH witnesses the injustice but appears to tolerate it. The verb rāʾâ ('to see') in this context means watching without intervening — the most maddening form of non-response. The petition hāšîbâ napšî ('restore my life') from šûb ('to bring back') — as if the soul has been taken or driven away and needs restoration — and yĕḥîdātî mikkĕpîrîm, 'my precious one from the young lions,' uses the same yĕḥîdâ ('precious one') as Ps 22:20.
18: ʾôdĕkā bĕqāhāl rāb bĕʿam ʿāṣûm ʾăhallĕlekā (ʾôḏĕḵā bĕqāhāl rāḇ bĕʿam ʿāṣûm ʾăhallĕleḵā): 'I will thank you in the great assembly; in the mighty throng I will praise you.' The vow of public praise (bĕqāhāl rāb, 'in the great congregation') is the psalmist's counter-offering to the enemies' feast (v.16): if they feast on his misfortune, he will make YHWH's deliverance a public feast of praise. The phrase bĕʿam ʿāṣûm ('in a mighty/numerous people') emphasizes the size of the audience — the vindication will be as public as the shame was. The praise is the response that transforms private rescue into communal testimony (cf. Ps 22:25).
19: ʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî ʾōyĕbay šeqer śōnĕʾay ḥinnām ʾal-yiqrĕṣû ʿāyin (ʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî ʾōyĕbay šeqer śōnĕʾay ḥinnām ʾal-yiqrĕṣû ʿāyin): 'Let not those rejoice over me who are wrongfully my foes, nor let those wink the eye who hate me without cause.' The key phrase śōnĕʾay ḥinnām ('those who hate me without cause') parallels v.7's ḥinnām — the unprovoked nature of the hatred is repeated. John 15:25 cites this phrase: 'They hated me without a cause,' applying the psalmist's experience of arbitrary persecution to Jesus. The gesture yiqrĕṣû ʿāyin ('wink the eye') is the gesture of hidden malice — the concealed pleasure of seeing another suffer, expressed through a private eye-movement that signals to confederates while maintaining a public face.
20: kî lōʾ šālôm yĕdabbĕrû wĕʿal ragʿê-ʾereṣ diwrê mirmôt yaḥăšōbûn (kî lōʾ šālôm yĕḏabbĕrû wĕʿal raḡʿê-ʾereṣ diḇrê mirmôṯ yaḥăšōḇûn): 'For they do not speak peace, but against those who are quiet in the land they devise words of deceit.' The contrast is between šālôm ('peace' — the content of righteous speech) and diwrê mirmôt ('words of deceit'). The target rĕgê hāʾāreṣ ('the quiet ones of the land') are those who live peaceably, uninvolved in conflict — precisely those who should be unmolested. The enemies target the peaceful: their predation is not retaliatory but predatory.
21: wayyarḥîbû ʿālay pîhem ʾāmĕrû heʾāḥ heʾāḥ rāʾătâ ʿênênû (wayyarḥîbû ʿālay pîhem ʾāmĕrû heʾāḥ heʾāḥ rāʾāṯâ ʿênênû): 'They open wide their mouths against me; they say, 'Aha, aha! Our eyes have seen it!'' The verb rāḥab ('to be wide, broad') in the hiphil wayyarḥîbû ('they opened wide') applied to the mouth describes the maximum exposure of mockery — the gaping maw of contempt (cf. Ps 22:13). The exclamation heʾāḥ heʾāḥ ('aha! aha!') is the vocalization of satisfied malice — the sound of enemies witnessing what they desired. The claim rāʾătâ ʿênênû ('our eyes have seen') is triumphant: they have witnessed the psalmist's downfall and now claim confirmation of their judgment against him.
22: rāʾîtâ YHWH ʾal-teḥĕraš ʾădōnāy ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî (rāʾîṯā YHWH ʾal-teḥĕraš ʾăḏōnāy ʾal-tirḥaq mimmennî): 'You have seen, O LORD; be not silent! O Lord, be not far from me!' The perfect rāʾîtâ ('you have seen') turns the enemies' boast (v.21, 'our eyes have seen') against itself: YHWH has also seen, and his seeing is the ground of hope. The negative imperatives ʾal-teḥĕraš ('do not be silent!') and ʾal-tirḥaq ('do not be far!') pray against two modes of divine abandonment: silence (non-speech) and distance (non-presence). The psalmist wants YHWH to convert his silent seeing into active speech and presence.
23: hāʿîrâ wĕhāqîṣâ lĕmišpāṭî ʾĕlōhay waʾdōnāy lĕrîbî (hāʿîrâ wĕhāqîṣâ lĕmišpāṭî ʾĕlōhay waʾḏōnāy lĕrîḇî): 'Awake and rouse yourself for my vindication, for my cause, my God and my Lord!' The imperatives hāʿîrâ ('awake!') and hāqîṣâ ('rouse yourself!') treat YHWH as sleeping — the bold anthropomorphism of Ps 44:23 ('awake! why are you sleeping, O Lord?'). The cause is lĕmišpāṭî lĕrîbî ('for my vindication, for my lawsuit') — legal categories. YHWH is summoned as judge (ʾĕlōhay, 'my God') and advocate (waʾdōnāy, 'my Lord') in a case where the enemies have brought false testimony (v.11).
24: šopĕṭēnî kĕṣidqĕkā YHWH ʾĕlōhāy wĕʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî (šop̄ĕṭēnî ḵĕṣiḏqĕḵā YHWH ʾĕlōhāy wĕʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî): 'Vindicate me, O LORD my God, according to your righteousness, and let them not rejoice over me!' The imperative šopĕṭēnî ('vindicate me, judge me') grounds the request in kĕṣidqĕkā ('according to your righteousness') — the psalmist appeals not to his own merit but to YHWH's covenant character. The prayer against rejoicing — wĕʾal-yiśmĕḥû-lî ('let them not rejoice over me') — returns to the opening petition (v.4: yiśmĕḥû ʿālay ʾōyĕbay). The framing concern is the enemy's gloating, which would be a public victory for wickedness over covenant righteousness.
25: ʾal-yōʾmĕrû bĕlibbām heʾāḥ napšēnû ʾal-yōʾmĕrû billa'ănûhû (ʾal-yōʾmĕrû bĕlibbām heʾāḥ napšēnû ʾal-yōʾmĕrû billa'ănûhû): 'Let them not say in their hearts, 'Aha, our soul's desire!' Let them not say, 'We have swallowed him up.'' The inner speech ʾal-yōʾmĕrû bĕlibbām ('let them not say in their hearts') returns to the enemies' self-talk. The exclamation heʾāḥ napšēnû ('aha, what our soul desired!') reveals the predatory appetite behind the attack: the psalmist's downfall is what the enemies' nepeš ('appetite, desire') wanted. The metaphor billa'ănûhû ('we have swallowed him up') pictures consumption — the psalmist as prey devoured whole.
26: yēbōšû wĕyikklĕmû yaḥdāw śĕmēḥê rāʿātî (yēḇōšû wĕyikkālĕmû yaḥdāw śĕmēḥê rāʿāṯî): 'Let them be put to shame and disappointed altogether who rejoice at my calamity.' The verb śāmaḥ ('rejoice') at rāʿātî ('my calamity') identifies the enemies by their pleasure in another's suffering — Schadenfreude as a theological category. The shame they sought to impose on the psalmist is redirected: yēbōšû ('let them be shamed') and yikklĕmû ('let them be disgraced'). The additional prayer yilbĕšû-bōšet ûkĕlimmâ hammitgaddĕlîm ʿālay, 'let those who magnify themselves against me be clothed with shame and dishonor' — clothing is a metaphor for complete encasement in identity: shame becomes their permanent garment.
27: yārōnnû wĕyiśmĕḥû ḥăpēṣê ṣidqî (yārōnnû wĕyiśmĕḥû ḥăp̄ēṣê ṣiḏqî): 'Let those who delight in my righteousness shout for joy and be glad.' Against the enemies who delight in calamity (v.26) stand those who delight in ṣidqî ('my righteousness/vindication'): the community that wants righteous outcomes for the righteous. The phrase wĕyōʾmĕrû tāmîd yigdal YHWH haḥāpēṣ šĕlôm ʿabdô, 'and let them say continually, 'Great is the LORD, who delights in the peace of his servant!'' — the desired outcome of rescue is communal praise. YHWH's delight in šālôm for his servant is the theological assertion that peace for the righteous is not incidental but purposeful divine will.
28: ûlĕšônî tehgeh ṣidqātĕkā kol-hayyôm tĕhillātekā (ûlĕšônî ṯehgeh ṣiḏqāṯĕḵā ḵol-hayyôm tĕhillāṯeḵā): 'Then my tongue shall tell of your righteousness and of your praise all the day long.' The concluding vow ûlĕšônî tehgeh ('and my tongue shall murmur/declare') from hāgâ ('to mutter, meditate, declare') — the word used in Ps 1:2 for meditating on Torah — closes the imprecatory psalm with praise. The objects ṣidqātĕkā ('your righteousness') and tĕhillātekā ('your praise') are the content of the ongoing declaration: YHWH's righteous vindication of the innocent becomes the substance of a lifetime of worship. The imprecatory prayer resolves not in violence but in testimony.
